body.text: This note is written as a source document for formalization. All groups are written multiplicatively unless an abelian group is explicitly written additively.

body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: For a group G with identity e, a function l : G → ℝ satisfying: for every g ∈ G, 0 ≤ l(g); l(e)=0; for every g ∈ G, l(g⁻¹)=l(g); for every g,h ∈ G, l(gh) ≤ l(g)+l(h).
body.name: PseudoLength

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: A pseudo-length function l : G → ℝ such that for every g ∈ G, g ≠ e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: A pseudo-length function l : G → ℝ such that for every g ∈ G and every n ∈ ℤ, l(g^n)=|n| l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

body.text: In particular, for every $g \in G$, homogeneity gives $l(g^2)=2l(g)$.

body.label: Definition 5
body.definition: The subgroup of G generated by the set {[x,y] | x,y ∈ G}.
body.name: commutatorSubgroup

body.label: Definition 6
body.definition: G_ab := G/[G,G], with π_ab : G → G_ab the quotient homomorphism.
body.name: abelianization

body.label: Definition 7
body.definition: For an abelian group A, T(A) := {a ∈ A | ∃ n ∈ ℤ, n ≠ 0 and n a = 0}.
body.name: torsionSubgroup

body.label: Definition 8
body.definition: An abelian group A such that T(A)={0}.
body.name: IsTorsionFreeAbelian

proof_steps.proof_method: The two inequalities imply equality.

proof.text:
Fix $x,y \in G$. We first prove $l(yxy^{-1}) \leq l(x)$.

Let $n$ be a positive integer. By homogeneity,
$$
n\,l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n).
$$
The group identity $(yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1}$ gives
$$
n\,l(yxy^{-1}) = l(yx^n y^{-1}).
$$
By the triangle inequality,
$$
l(yx^n y^{-1}) \leq l(y) + l(x^n) + l(y^{-1}).
$$
By symmetry, $l(y^{-1})=l(y)$. By homogeneity, $l(x^n)=n\,l(x)$. Hence
$$
n\,l(yxy^{-1}) \leq 2l(y)+n\,l(x).
$$
Dividing by the positive real number $n$ gives
$$
l(yxy^{-1}) \leq l(x)+\frac{2l(y)}{n}.
$$
Since this inequality holds for every positive integer $n$, and $2l(y)/n$ tends to $0$, it follows that
$$
l(yxy^{-1}) \leq l(x).
$$

For the reverse inequality, define $z := yxy^{-1}$. Then $x=y^{-1}zy$. Applying the inequality already proved with $z$ in place of $x$ and $y^{-1}$ in place of $y$ gives
$$
l(x)=l(y^{-1}zy) \leq l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}).
$$
The two inequalities imply $l(yxy^{-1})=l(x)$.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the induction hypothesis.
Combining the preceding equality and inequalities and simplifying algebraically.

induction_step_proof.text:
Assume the inequality holds for a natural number n. The definition gives
C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}.
By Lemma 1, applied to yC_nz and w,
l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz).
By two applications of the triangle inequality,
l(yC_nz) \leq l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z).
By the induction hypothesis,
l(C_n) \leq l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
Combining gives
l(C_{n+1}) \leq l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)).

proof.text:
We prove the claim by induction on $n$.

For $n=0$, the definition gives $C_0=s^{-1}t$. By the triangle inequality,
$$
l(C_0)=l(s^{-1}t) \leq l(s^{-1})+l(t).
$$
This is the desired inequality for $n=0$.

Assume the inequality holds for a natural number $n$. The definition gives
$$
C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}.
$$
Equivalently,
$$
C_{n+1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}.
$$
By Lemma 1, applied to the element $yC_nz$ and the conjugating element $w$,
$$
l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz).
$$
By two applications of the triangle inequality,
$$
l(yC_nz) \leq l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z).
$$
By the induction hypothesis,
$$
l(C_n) \leq l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
$$
Combining the last two inequalities gives
$$
l(C_{n+1})
\leq l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)).
$$
This proves the induction step.

Therefore the inequality holds for every natural number $n$.

proof_steps.proof_method: Since the inequality holds for every positive integer n and (l(s)+l(t))/n tends to 0, let n tend to infinity.

proof.text:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. From the two hypotheses and the standard identity for powers of conjugates, we have
$$
a^n=s(wy)^n s^{-1}
$$
and
$$
a^n=t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}.
$$
Hence
$$
a^{2n}=a^n a^n
      =s(wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}.
$$
By homogeneity,
$$
2n\,l(a)=l(a^{2n}).
$$
Using the displayed expression for $a^{2n}$ and the triangle inequality,
$$
l(a^{2n})
\leq l(s)+l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n)+l(t^{-1}).
$$
By Lemma 2,
$$
l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n)
\leq l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
$$
By symmetry, $l(s^{-1})=l(s)$ and $l(t^{-1})=l(t)$. Therefore
$$
2n\,l(a) \leq 2l(s)+2l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
$$
Dividing by $2n$ gives
$$
l(a) \leq \frac{l(y)+l(z)}{2}+\frac{l(s)+l(t)}{n}.
$$
Since this inequality holds for every positive integer $n$, and $(l(s)+l(t))/n$ tends to $0$, we obtain
$$
l(a) \leq \frac{l(y)+l(z)}{2}.
$$

body.text: The next lemma is the combinatorial input used to prove that homogeneous pseudo-length functions vanish on commutators. It is stated as a separate lemma because its proof is a finite calculation in the group laws followed by Lemma 3.

body.label: Lemma 4
body.claim: Let G be a group, l : G → ℝ a homogeneous pseudo-length function, x,y ∈ G, c:=[x,y], and f : ℤ×ℤ → ℝ be defined by f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). For every m,k ∈ ℤ, f(m,k) ≤ (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.

proof_steps.assumption: m,k ∈ ℤ are fixed arbitrary integers.

proof_steps.statement: a := x^m c^k
w := x
v := y⁻¹x^m c^(k-1)xy

proof_steps.proof_method: Expand the definitions of w,u,a and multiply powers of x.
Since a = wu, a is conjugate to wu by the identity conjugation.
Substitute w = x into vw⁻¹ and expand the definition of v.
Rewrite the commutator identity c = xyx⁻¹y⁻¹ by multiplying on the right by y.
Use xyx⁻¹ = cy, combine powers of c, and substitute a = x^m c^k.
Rearrange the equality vw⁻¹ = y⁻¹ay by conjugating with y.
The displayed equality expresses a as a conjugate of vw⁻¹.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If a=s(wy)s⁻¹ and a=t(zw⁻¹)t⁻¹, then l(a) ≤ (l(y)+l(z))/2.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 3
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied with s=e, t=y, y=u, and z=v.

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 3 with s=e, t=y, y=u, and z=v, using the established conjugacy identities.
Expand the definition of u and then use the definition of f.
Expand v and cancel yy⁻¹ terms.
Multiply powers of x and cancel xx⁻¹ as displayed.
The two displayed conjugations show that v is conjugate to x^(m+1)c^(k-1).

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For every x,y ∈ G, l(yxy⁻¹)=l(x).
deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 1
deduced_from_theorem.description: Used to conclude that conjugate elements have the same l-value.

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 1 to the conjugate elements v and x^(m+1)c^(k-1), then use the definition of f.
Expand the definition of a and use the definition of f.
Substitute the formulas for l(a), l(u), and l(v) into the inequality obtained from Lemma 3.

proof.text:
Fix $m,k \in \mathbb{Z}$. Define
$$
a:=x^m c^k,
$$
$$
w:=x,
$$
$$
u:=x^{m-1}c^k,
$$
and
$$
v:=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xy.
$$
Then
$$
wu=x(x^{m-1}c^k)=x^m c^k=a.
$$
Thus $a$ is conjugate to $wu$, because $a=wu$.

We next prove that $a$ is conjugate to $vw^{-1}$. Since $w=x$,
$$
vw^{-1}=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xyx^{-1}.
$$
Using the commutator identity
$$
c=xyx^{-1}y^{-1},
$$
we have
$$
xyx^{-1}=cy.
$$
Therefore
$$
vw^{-1}
=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}cy
=y^{-1}x^m c^k y
=y^{-1}ay.
$$
Hence $a=y(vw^{-1})y^{-1}$, so $a$ is conjugate to $vw^{-1}$.

Lemma 3, applied with $s=e$, $t=y$, $y=u$, and $z=v$, gives
$$
l(a)\leq \frac{l(u)+l(v)}{2}.
$$
By the definition of $u$,
$$
l(u)=l(x^{m-1}c^k)=f(m-1,k).
$$
It remains to identify $l(v)$. The element $v$ is conjugate to $x^{m+1}c^{k-1}$ because
$$
yvy^{-1}=x^m c^{k-1}x
$$
and
$$
x(x^m c^{k-1}x)x^{-1}=x^{m+1}c^{k-1}.
$$
By Lemma 1, conjugate elements have the same length. Therefore
$$
l(v)=l(x^{m+1}c^{k-1})=f(m+1,k-1).
$$
Substituting the formulas for $l(u)$ and $l(v)$ into the inequality from Lemma 3 gives
$$
f(m,k)\leq \frac{f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1)}{2}.
$$

body.claim: Let G be a group, l : G → ℝ a homogeneous pseudo-length function, x,y ∈ G, c:=[x,y], and f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). Let n be a natural number, let ε₁,…,ε₂ₙ be independent Rademacher random variables, and set S₂ₙ:=ε₁+⋯+ε₂ₙ. Then f(0,n) ≤ 𝔼[f(S₂ₙ,-S₂ₙ/2)].

proof_steps.proof_method: Start at (0,n) and apply the preceding one-step inequality successively with eps_1,…,eps_{2n}.
By the definitions of M_{2n} and S_{2n}.
Expand the sum and use ∑_{i=1}^{2n} eps_i = S_{2n}.
Algebraic simplification.
Substitute the simplified expression for the sum into the definition of K_{2n}.
Substitute the formulas for M_{2n} and K_{2n} into the iterated inequality.

proof.text:
Lemma 4 says that, for every $m,k \in \mathbb{Z}$,
$$
f(m,k)\leq
\frac{1}{2}f(m-1,k)+\frac{1}{2}f(m+1,k-1).
$$
Let $\epsilon$ be a Rademacher random variable. The preceding inequality is equivalent to
$$
f(m,k)\leq
\mathbb{E}\bigl[f(m+\epsilon,k-(1+\epsilon)/2)\bigr].
$$
Indeed, the case $\epsilon=-1$ gives $(m-1,k)$, and the case $\epsilon=1$ gives $(m+1,k-1)$.

Starting at $(0,n)$ and applying the preceding inequality successively with $\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_{2n}$ gives
$$
f(0,n)\leq
\mathbb{E}\bigl[f(M_{2n},K_{2n})\bigr],
$$
where
$$
M_{2n}:=\epsilon_1+\cdots+\epsilon_{2n}
$$
and
$$
K_{2n}:=n-\sum_{i=1}^{2n}\frac{1+\epsilon_i}{2}.
$$
The first coordinate is $M_{2n}=S_{2n}$. Also,
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{2n}\frac{1+\epsilon_i}{2}
=\frac{2n+S_{2n}}{2}
=n+\frac{S_{2n}}{2}.
$$
Therefore $K_{2n}=-S_{2n}/2$. Substituting these formulas for $M_{2n}$ and $K_{2n}$ gives
$$
f(0,n)\leq \mathbb{E}\bigl[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)\bigr].
$$

proof.text:
By Jensen's inequality applied to the concave function $u\mapsto \sqrt{u}$ on $[0,\infty)$,
$$
\mathbb{E}(|S_{2n}|)
=\mathbb{E}\bigl(\sqrt{S_{2n}^2}\bigr)
\leq \sqrt{\mathbb{E}(S_{2n}^2)}.
$$
The variables $\epsilon_i$ have mean $0$ and variance $1$. Since the $\epsilon_i$ are independent,
$$
\mathbb{E}(S_{2n}^2)=2n.
$$
Therefore $\mathbb{E}(|S_{2n}|)\leq\sqrt{2n}$.

proof.text:
Fix $x,y \in G$ and define $c:=[x,y]$. Let $n$ be a positive integer. By homogeneity,
$$
n\,l(c)=l(c^n)=f(0,n),
$$
where $f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k)$.

By Lemma 5,
$$
f(0,n)\leq \mathbb{E}\bigl[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)\bigr].
$$
For every even integer $r$,
$$
f(r,-r/2)=l(x^r c^{-r/2}).
$$
By the triangle inequality,
$$
l(x^r c^{-r/2})\leq l(x^r)+l(c^{-r/2}).
$$
By homogeneity,
$$
l(x^r)=|r|\,l(x)
$$
and
$$
l(c^{-r/2})=|r|\,l(c)/2.
$$
Therefore
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)\bigr]
\leq \mathbb{E}(|S_{2n}|)\,(l(x)+l(c)/2).
$$
By Lemma 6,
$$
\mathbb{E}(|S_{2n}|)\leq\sqrt{2n}.
$$
Combining these inequalities gives
$$
n\,l(c)\leq \sqrt{2n}\,(l(x)+l(c)/2).
$$
Dividing by the positive real number $n$ gives
$$
l(c)\leq \frac{\sqrt{2n}}{n}\,(l(x)+l(c)/2).
$$
The right-hand side tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity. Hence $l(c)\leq 0$. By nonnegativity of the pseudo-length function, $0\leq l(c)$. Thus $l(c)=0$.

Since $x$ and $y$ were arbitrary, $l([x,y])=0$ for all $x,y \in G$.

proof.text:
Define $\bar l(\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(g)):=l(g)$ for $g\in G$. We first prove that this definition is independent of the chosen representative.

Assume $\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(g)=\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(h)$. Then $h^{-1}g\in [G,G]$. By Lemma 8, $l(h^{-1}g)=0$. Since $g=h(h^{-1}g)$, the triangle inequality gives
$$
l(g)\leq l(h)+l(h^{-1}g)=l(h).
$$
Similarly, $g^{-1}h\in [G,G]$, and the triangle inequality applied to $h=g(g^{-1}h)$ gives $l(h)\leq l(g)$. Hence $l(g)=l(h)$.
Thus $\bar l$ is well-defined.

The identity $l=\bar l\circ\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}$ holds by definition of $\bar l$. Since $\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}$ is surjective, this identity also proves uniqueness of $\bar l$.

The pseudo-length axioms for $\bar l$ follow from the corresponding axioms for $l$ and from the fact that $\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}$ is a group homomorphism.
For example, if $a=\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(g)$ and $b=\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(h)$, then
$$
\bar l(ab)=\bar l(\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}(gh))=l(gh)
\leq l(g)+l(h)=\bar l(a)+\bar l(b).
$$
The proofs of nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and homogeneity are analogous.

proof.text:
By Lemma 9, $l$ factors through a homogeneous pseudo-length function
$$
\bar l:G_{\mathrm{ab}}\to\mathbb{R}.
$$
By Lemma 11, $\bar l$ factors through the torsion-free quotient
$$
A:=G_{\mathrm{ab}}/T(G_{\mathrm{ab}}).
$$
Let
$$
\tilde l:A\to\mathbb{R}
$$
be the induced homogeneous pseudo-length function.

By Lemma 12, $\tilde l$ extends to a seminorm on
$$
V_{\mathbb Q}:=A\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q.
$$
By Lemma 13, there exist a real Banach space $B$ and a canonical map $V_{\mathbb Q}\to B$ preserving the seminorm.

Define $\phi:G\to B$ to be the composite
$$
G
\xrightarrow{\pi_{\mathrm{ab}}}
G_{\mathrm{ab}}
\to
A
\to
V_{\mathbb Q}
\to
B.
$$
Each arrow in this composite is a group homomorphism when $B$ is regarded as an additive group. Hence $\phi$ is a group homomorphism. By construction, for every $g\in G$,
$$
l(g)=\|\phi(g)\|_B.
$$

Assume now that $l$ is a length function. We prove that $\phi$ is injective. Let $g\in G$ and assume $\phi(g)=0$. Then
$$
l(g)=\|\phi(g)\|_B=\|0\|_B=0.
$$
Since $l$ is a length function, $l(g)=0$ implies $g=e$. Therefore the kernel of $\phi$ is trivial, and $\phi$ is injective.
